symbols: Jesus kneeling to wash feet — the King of Kings assuming the role of the lowest servant
basin and water — cleansing, purification, and the washing away of sin
towel girded around Christ — the garment of service replacing the garment of glory
Peter's resistant gesture — human pride confronted by divine humility
halo around Christ's head — divine identity maintained even in the posture of a slave
description: An interior scene in an upper room with a white-clothed table at which several disciples sit. In the foreground center, Jesus kneels before Peter, washing his foot over a copper basin. Jesus has a golden halo and wears a simple greenish-brown tunic. Peter, an elderly bearded man in a green robe, sits on a bench with his hands clasped in an emotional gesture of protest or submission. Other disciples observe from the table behind. A red basin of water sits on the floor, and sandals are scattered nearby. The lighting warmly illuminates the central action.
theological_description: The foot washing, recorded in John 13:1-17, is one of the most profound demonstrations of servant leadership in all of Scripture. On the night before his crucifixion, Jesus 'got up from the meal, took off his outer clothing, and wrapped a towel around his waist. After that, he poured water into a basin and began to wash his disciples' feet' (John 13:4-5). When Peter protested — 'You shall never wash my feet' — Jesus replied, 'Unless I wash you, you have no part with me' (John 13:8). This act overturns all human notions of power and authority. The one whom John identifies as the Word through whom all things were made (John 1:3) kneels as a household slave before fishermen. Paul describes this kenotic movement: Christ, 'being in very nature God, did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage; rather, he made himself nothing by taking the very nature of a servant' (Philippians 2:6-7). Jesus then commands his followers: 'I have set you an example that you should do as I have done for you' (John 13:15). The foot washing thus becomes both sacramental act and ethical mandate — the pattern of all Christian leadership and love.

scripture_refs.reason: Jesus washing Peter's feet and Peter's protest — the exact narrative depicted
Jesus commanding the disciples to wash one another's feet as he has done for them
Christ's self-emptying and taking the form of a servant — visually enacted in the foot washing
The Son of Man came not to be served but to serve — the theological principle behind the scene
